jokes.text: Oh, why can't they just call it May 5? This is what I was talking about. It's all part of the Mexican war on May 5.
jokes.setup: Lucille is upset about her maid taking off for Cinco de Mayo
jokes.punchline: She frames a national holiday as a personal attack on the date 'May 5'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Lucille - turning a cultural celebration into persecution of a calendar date shows her narcissistic worldview

jokes.text: You see, the Hispanics of Newport liked Cinco a lot, but high up on Coast Highway, Lucille Bluth did not.
jokes.setup: The narrator is telling the origin story of Cinco de Cuatro
jokes.punchline: Direct parody of 'How the Grinch Stole Christmas' with racist undertones
jokes.explanation: Clever literary reference that perfectly captures Lucille's petty antagonism toward a cultural celebration

jokes.text: And then Lucille had a horrible thought, a thought that was thoughtless and better to not. 'What if,' she thought with a sneering grin... Next year what if the party didn't even begin?
jokes.setup: Continuing the Grinch parody
jokes.punchline: The narrator maintains the Dr. Seuss rhyme scheme while describing Lucille's plot
jokes.explanation: Sustaining the literary parody while advancing the plot - shows commitment to the bit

jokes.text: Fill the bay with chorizo. Chorizo dump!
jokes.setup: Lucille is plotting to sabotage Cinco de Mayo celebrations
jokes.punchline: The completely impractical and wasteful solution of dumping chorizo in the bay
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation that shows the Bluths' tendency toward expensive, ineffective solutions

jokes.text: Festival of Lights-- another phrase they wanted back
jokes.setup: Describing the boat parade name
jokes.punchline: The implication that the Bluths are trying to reclaim 'Festival of Lights' from Jewish culture
jokes.explanation: Subtle dig at the family's casual cultural appropriation and entitlement

jokes.text: She certainly had demonstrated that with many of the men in the family... Oh, stop it, you're making me dizzy. ...even having fallen for a few, although some of that was the vertigo she suffered from. But not all of it.
jokes.setup: Narrator explaining Lucille 2's romantic history with the Bluth men
jokes.punchline: The double meaning of 'fallen for' - romantically and literally due to vertigo
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that works on multiple levels and builds character continuity

jokes.text: Dad? No, no. But seriously, she had gotten intimate with Buster and Gob.
jokes.setup: The narrator is explaining Lucille 2's romantic connections
jokes.punchline: Michael's horrified reaction to learning about his family members' intimate relationships
jokes.explanation: Good cringe moment that builds on established character relationships and Michael's disgust

jokes.text: What are you doing? I'm gonna have sex with you for money.
jokes.setup: Michael is desperately trying to get money from Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: Michael bluntly states his prostitution plan with shocking directness
jokes.explanation: Shocking character moment - Michael's moral compass completely breaks down, delivered with brutal honesty

jokes.text: Thanks for coming so late, Maria, I know that you won't be cleaning up tomorrow, and we did make quite a mess of that bedroom.
jokes.setup: Michael unexpectedly encounters Gob in the model home
jokes.punchline: Gob's casual reference to his sexual encounter with the maid
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate inappropriate oversharing from Gob

jokes.text: Well, I wasn't hoping to help. I was hoping to rub it in because of all the unpleasantness.
jokes.setup: Michael admits he needs help and did something shameful
jokes.punchline: Gob's honest admission that he wants to make Michael feel worse
jokes.explanation: Perfect Gob - brutally honest about his petty motivations

jokes.text: Bad example: if you were ashamed of being in love with a man, suddenly discovered these new feelings, then I might say something like, 'Homo much?' It's not that. I said 'Bad example.'
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to figure out what Michael is ashamed of
jokes.punchline: Gob gives a terrible example of mockery, then immediately clarifies it's a bad example
jokes.explanation: Meta-joke where the character acknowledges his own bad joke while still delivering it

jokes.text: It's almost as if he found a way to simulate amnesia. Hey, I'm down to my last Forget-Me-Now.
jokes.setup: Narrator explaining Gob's selective memory about embarrassing encounters
jokes.punchline: Visual of Gob holding what's clearly a roofie pill, casually named 'Forget-Me-Now'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback that recontextualizes Gob's casual drug use as a coping mechanism

jokes.text: Gob had always taken a pill he called a Forget-Me-Now for that... but was commonly known as a roofie.
jokes.setup: Explaining Gob's pill habit
jokes.punchline: Casually revealing he's been using date rape drugs on himself
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with narrator's trademark matter-of-fact tone, making the horrible casual

jokes.text: ♪ It's so easy to forget... ♪ ♪ It's so easy to forget. ♪ Stupid, forgetful Michael.
jokes.setup: Gob forces Michael to take a Forget-Me-Now pill
jokes.punchline: Gob sings while Michael forgets, then calls him 'forgetful' for forgetting
jokes.explanation: Ironic that Gob calls Michael forgetful when Gob just drugged him to forget

jokes.text: even though the Phoenix he went to was online, he still enjoyed the full college experience by living in a dorm room while doing so
jokes.setup: Michael threatened to go to Phoenix to finish school
jokes.punchline: He went to 'Phoenix' - University of Phoenix online - but still lives in a dorm
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion - Michael technically kept his promise but in the most pathetic way possible

jokes.text: Unfortunately it was his son's dorm room at the University of California at Irvine.
jokes.setup: Michael is living in a dorm room for his college experience
jokes.punchline: It's his son's dorm room, making it incredibly awkward
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that makes Michael's situation exponentially more pathetic

jokes.text: We know everything there is to know about each other. We're like twins. I don't see us as twins.
jokes.setup: Michael claims he and his son have no secrets
jokes.punchline: George Michael immediately contradicts the 'twins' comparison
jokes.explanation: Shows the growing disconnect between Michael's perception and reality of their relationship

jokes.text: Well, it's hard to be self-effacing when you're a perfect specimen of rugged manlessness.
jokes.setup: Michael wants George Michael to be more self-effacing
jokes.punchline: George Michael's oxymoronic description of himself as 'rugged manlessness'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant wordplay that perfectly captures George Michael's awkward self-awareness

jokes.text: This guy's name is actually George Michael. No, it's two different guys. They're twins, ugly. We should sign them up for Twin Club.
jokes.setup: Campus housing is confused by George Michael's name
jokes.punchline: They assume it's two people and that they're ugly twins
jokes.explanation: Simple misunderstanding joke that explains the large room situation

jokes.text: It was in this men's room in-- get this-- Beverly Hills that singer George Michael was arrested for soliciting sex from an undercover cop.
jokes.setup: George Michael googles his own name
jokes.punchline: He finds news about the singer George Michael's arrest for public lewdness
jokes.explanation: Real-world reference that explains George Michael's desire to change his name

jokes.text: Right. I got it, let's call you Boy George.
jokes.setup: George Michael wants to avoid the singer association
jokes.punchline: Michael suggests another problematic singer name
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and immediate punchline - Michael completely misses the point

jokes.text: Singer Boy George in the news again today, this time for sexual assault in a men's public bathroom.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests 'Boy George' as an alternative name
jokes.punchline: Immediate news report about Boy George's similar scandal
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing - the universe immediately punishes Michael's suggestion

jokes.text: I mean, at least it was consensual.
jokes.setup: Comparing the George Michael and Boy George scandals
jokes.punchline: George Michael finds the silver lining in public indecency vs assault
jokes.explanation: Darkly funny rationalization that shows George Michael's twisted optimism

jokes.text: Listen, look it. Are you listening? You want your space. I get it.
jokes.setup: George Michael tries to explain about privacy software
jokes.punchline: Michael's desperate verbal scrambling shows his hurt feelings
jokes.explanation: Michael's emotional fragility comes through in his stammering speech pattern

jokes.text: You know what? This is your school rally shirt. You know, same size--medium. Do you know where mine is? We wear the same size like twi... We're just like twin-- couple of identical...
jokes.setup: Michael is packing to leave
jokes.punchline: He keeps trying to say they're like twins while proving they're not
jokes.explanation: Michael's desperation to maintain closeness while George Michael pulls away

jokes.text: It's privacy software. That's why the word came up.
jokes.setup: Michael thinking George Michael wants privacy from him
jokes.punchline: George Michael desperately claiming it was just about his software project
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing George Michael's conflict avoidance and quick thinking under pressure

jokes.text: It's, like, stuck on 2003. Help me just break into... You just hit 'today.' The 'today' button. Sorry. It's stuck in 2003, so I don't have 'today.'
jokes.setup: Michael can't figure out his calendar
jokes.punchline: He insists there's no 'today' button because his calendar is stuck in 2003
jokes.explanation: Perfect example of Michael's technological incompetence and stubbornness

jokes.text: Oh, okay. Kind of like the antisocial network, right? That's very good. You can use it.
jokes.setup: George Michael explains Faceblock as the opposite of Facebook
jokes.punchline: Michael comes up with 'antisocial network' and generously gives George Michael permission to use it
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay plus Michael's patronizing attitude toward his son's work

jokes.text: Next to SkyMall, it's, like, the number two most read in all of coach. Well, three with the safety card
jokes.setup: Michael brags about being in Altitude magazine
jokes.punchline: He keeps downgrading its importance as he talks
jokes.explanation: Michael accidentally undermines his own achievement while trying to boast

jokes.text: Okay, give me your meal plan card. I'm gonna run in there, I'm gonna grab a tray, you run around back, make sure you get there in time, before my hand stamp dries.
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael are getting food
jokes.punchline: Michael has a complex scheme to scam the cafeteria using George Michael's meal plan
jokes.explanation: Shows Michael's desperation and willingness to involve his son in petty schemes

jokes.text: How'd you like to be bumped up to Lucille 1?
jokes.setup: Stan Sitwell is selling his shares back to Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: Lucille 2 suggests replacing Lucille as the primary Lucille
jokes.explanation: Clever play on their shared name and Lucille 2's ambition

jokes.text: headed out on whatever the opposite of a maiden voyage is
jokes.setup: Lucille flees on a boat to avoid prosecution
jokes.punchline: The narrator struggles to find the opposite of 'maiden voyage' for an older woman
jokes.explanation: Clever linguistic observation that highlights the gendered nature of nautical terms

jokes.text: Well, I missed the hearing. Oh, it just snuck up on me, and then they called, and they wanted me there at eight bells. You know, I thought that was their 5:00 p.m.
jokes.setup: Lucille has missed her maritime law hearing
jokes.punchline: She confused nautical time with regular time
jokes.explanation: Typical Lucille - creating problems through willful ignorance

jokes.text: It seems Michael had been interested in maritime law since playing a lawyer in an original school play titled The Trial of Captain Hook. ♪ You're a crook, Captain Hook... ♪
jokes.setup: Michael is explaining maritime law to his mother
jokes.punchline: Flashback to young Michael in a terrible school play
jokes.explanation: Brilliant origin story for Michael's maritime law knowledge - a children's play

jokes.text: Watch out for the seal! Loose seal! Loose seal! I'm watching! You're out of the play!
jokes.setup: Young Michael is performing as a lawyer in the play
jokes.punchline: He warns about a loose seal (stage equipment) and gets kicked out
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for the series' 'loose seal' running gag - originated in childhood trauma

jokes.text: So the family got a little stimmy. Don't make a federal case out of it. That is exactly what they're going to make out of it.
jokes.setup: George Sr. casually mentions the stimulus money
jokes.punchline: Michael points out that fraud literally will become a federal case
jokes.explanation: Perfect literal interpretation of a figure of speech - will literally be a federal case

jokes.text: It's like tipping an African-American. You wouldn't tip an African-American, would you? Of course I would. On a train? What... Did you know about all this? No, I've been tipping them.
jokes.setup: Lucille explains why they didn't include Michael in the stimulus fraud
jokes.punchline: Bizarre racist analogy that confuses everyone, including George Sr. who's been 'tipping them'
jokes.explanation: Typical Bluth casual racism that exposes their confused worldview

jokes.text: Even a waiter? Okay, the treadmill's up and running.
jokes.setup: Confused conversation about tipping
jokes.punchline: Gob completely changes topics to home improvements
jokes.explanation: Typical Bluth family conversation - complete non sequitur that abandons the previous topic

jokes.text: And although his mother's day in court was something both he and his college-bound son would miss, Michael truly felt that nothing could go wrong. And then something did.
jokes.setup: The narrator says Michael felt nothing could go wrong
jokes.punchline: Immediately followed by 'And then something did'
jokes.explanation: Classic dramatic irony setup - telegraphing disaster right after optimism

jokes.text: This is why you don't confide in a competitor.
jokes.setup: Michael sees a competitor's successful development
jokes.punchline: Michael blames his failure on sharing information rather than market forces
jokes.explanation: Michael's refusal to accept responsibility for obvious market conditions

jokes.text: I got you! Just as he could count on her putting him off and falling down. You did. Bye-bye. Oop. That's a hot mess.
jokes.setup: Michael goes to Lucille 2 for money
jokes.punchline: The narrator's wordplay on 'putting him off' as both rejection and her falling down
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that works on multiple levels with established character trait

jokes.text: Love each other. Pete! Pete! Wait here. Hang on, Pete! Pete died.
jokes.setup: Michael is talking to George Michael about family
jokes.punchline: Casual mention that Pete the mailman died
jokes.explanation: Dark humor in the casual delivery of tragic news amid other complaints

jokes.text: Don't go through my stuff, Granddad. Hey, who do you think paid for your stuff?
jokes.setup: Michael is looking for George Michael in the computer lab
jokes.punchline: Another family dealing with multi-generational living arrangements and the same conflicts
jokes.explanation: Shows Michael's situation isn't unique in the economic downturn

jokes.text: Boy, shoot me if I'm 86, living in my grandson's dorm, huh?
jokes.setup: Michael sees another grandfather living in a dorm
jokes.punchline: Michael mocks the exact situation he's in, just one generation off
jokes.explanation: Perfect dramatic irony - Michael lacks self-awareness about his own situation

jokes.text: You're in Attitude? That magazine is gay. Oh, he wasn't being flip with the word 'gay.' Attitude is a British magazine for homosexuals.
jokes.setup: Michael mentions being in Altitude magazine
jokes.punchline: P-Hound mishears it as Attitude, and the narrator clarifies he literally meant gay
jokes.explanation: Clever misdirection that addresses assumptions about word usage

jokes.text: You ever even been on a plane, you piece of?
jokes.setup: P-Hound doubts the magazine exists
jokes.punchline: Michael's angry insult cuts off mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: Shows Michael's frustration and his attempt to maintain some decorum while insulting

jokes.text: There's nobody in here. Buddy, bad news. P-Hound is yanking our chain again. Uncle Michael. Oh, my God. Hi. Hello there. There is a kind of girl here.
jokes.setup: Michael walks in on what he thinks is George Michael with a girl
jokes.punchline: It's actually his cousin Maeby, making it 'kind of' a girl situation but not romantic
jokes.explanation: Awkward family dynamics with the incest undertones the show is known for

jokes.text: Won't that make it a tie? Briefly. Hmm? I mean, if the two of us vote against P-Hound, and you and P-Hound vote against you, then it's two against two, right?
jokes.setup: Michael has a complex voting scheme to remove P-Hound
jokes.punchline: George Michael immediately spots the mathematical flaw in Michael's 'foolproof' plan
jokes.explanation: Shows George Michael is smarter than his father and that Michael's plans always have obvious flaws

jokes.text: Did you mean to write 'George Michael'? Obviously, the verdict was in.
jokes.setup: Michael reads three votes against himself
jokes.punchline: He hopefully asks if the fourth vote was a mistake, but the narrator confirms his total defeat
jokes.explanation: Devastating moment where Michael realizes even his son voted against him - perfect escalation

jokes.text: I was mistakenly voted out of a four-person housing situation, uh, in a pack-first, no-talking-after scenario. Does that clear it up for you?
jokes.setup: Michael tries to explain why he has luggage but no flight
jokes.punchline: His overly specific explanation of being voted out of a dorm room
jokes.explanation: Michael's formal, businesslike description of his humiliating personal situation

jokes.text: But that leaves three other votes. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Michael explains he voted against himself
jokes.punchline: The employee immediately spots that means everyone else voted against him too
jokes.explanation: Stranger immediately understands the math that Michael can't face - brutal external perspective

jokes.text: Oh, okay, yeah, sure, we just, uh, let people walk onto the plane all the time. Do you want a box cutter also?
jokes.setup: Michael wants to board a plane just to get a magazine
jokes.punchline: Employee sarcastically references post-9/11 airport security
jokes.explanation: Sharp sarcasm that highlights how absurd Michael's request is in a post-9/11 world

jokes.text: And Michael kicked himself for a pose he thought would express humility to Sanjiv, the Altitude photographer.
jokes.setup: Michael finally gets the magazine with his article
jokes.punchline: The photo shows Michael in an embarrassingly submissive pose
jokes.explanation: Michael's attempt at humility backfires visually in the magazine photo

jokes.text: Hope you know a good place to live because I'm... Ooh, dear Lord. 'Cause I'm looking to make a new start. Don't touch that handle. Whoa, Mother of God! Dear Lord! Whoo!
jokes.setup: Michael arrives in Phoenix with hope for a new start
jokes.punchline: The extreme heat immediately overwhelms him
jokes.explanation: Classic fish-out-of-water moment as California Michael meets Arizona reality

jokes.text: Buster. No... no, Gob. Oh! It was Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Michael finds someone sleeping in the model home
jokes.punchline: He keeps guessing wrong about which family member it is
jokes.explanation: Shows that all the Bluth family members have ended up in the same desperate situation
